coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of tungsten-doped carbonated polymer dots with high sensitivity to Fe (III) and pH environments
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Yushu
Bao, Rui
Yi, Jianhong
Li, Hongdong
Liu, Liang
Huang, Shuyu
Li, Zhaojie
Zhang, Wenfu
Min, Deqi
coredata.dc:description: Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) have drawn a lot of attention in the past ten years because of their excellent selectivity and sensitivity. In this study, tungsten was doped into CPDs (W-CPDs) using a simple hydrothermal technique for the first time. With an excitation/emission maximum of 365/455 nm and excitation-independent emission, W-CPDs exhibit blue fluorescence and have an average particle diameter of 2.33 nm. Because of the dynamic quenching between Fe (III) and W-CPDs, there is a good linear relationship between the Fe (III) concentration and the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.012 μM for W-CPD fluorescence intensity. The intensity of the W-CPDs fluorescence is also found to be linearly correlated with pH values between 5.0 and 10.0. This technique enables quick detection of Fe (III) in drinking water and is expected to lower the risk of Fe (III) ingestion by humans. In addition, the creation of fluorescent flexible devices and information encryption are fascinating applications for W-CPDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tungsten
Carbonized polymer dots
Photoluminescence
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of Fe (III) ions in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

Yunnan Provincial Science and Technology Department

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52064032 , 52174345 ), the Yunnan Science and Technology Projects (Grant No. 202202AG050004 , 202002AB080001 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of W-CPDs (inset: the particle size distribution). (b) HRTEM image of the W-CPDs. (c) Raman spectra. (d) FT-IR spectra of W-CPDs and CPDs. (e) XPS survey of the W-CPDs and CPDs. (f) C 1 s, (g) N 1 s, (h) O 1 s, and (i) W 4 f XPS spectra.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of W-CPDs and CPDs (inset: from left to right are photos of W-CPDs and CPDs in daylight and 365 nm ultraviolet lights). (b) Excitation and Emission spectra of W-CPDs and CPDs. (c) 3D Excitation-dependent emission spectra of W-CPDs. (d) Zeta potentials of W-CPDs and CPDs.

(a) Relative fluorescence intensity of aqueous W-CPDs in presence of 12 μM different metal ions at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. (inset: photos of W-CPDs from top to bottom with different metal ions in visible and 365 nm ultraviolet light, respectively.) (b) Emission spectra of W-CPDs in the presence of different concentration of Fe (III) (Inset: the linear relationship between PL intensity and Fe (III) concentration in the range of 0.2 μM and 1.0 μM). (c) Time-resolved fluorescence decay of W-CPDs and CPDs without Fe (III), and Time-resolved fluorescence decay of W-CPDs with Fe (III) (12 μM). (d) Emission spectra of CPDs in the presence of different concentration of Fe (III) (Inset: the linear relationship between PL intensity and Fe (III) concentration in the range of 0.2 μM and 1.0 μM).
(a) W-CPDs growth mechanism. (b) W-O bond. (c) energy level of W-CPDs.

(a) PL spectra of the W-CPDs dispersed in solutions with different pH values (from 3.0 to 12.0) with excitation at 365 nm. (b) A linear relationship between PL intensity and pH values (from 5.0 to 10.0).

(a) Emission spectra of W-CPDs, (b) image of W-CPDs under natural light, (c) and images of W-CPDs under 365 nm UV light at day 1, day 7, day 15, and day 30, respectively. (d) Writing alphabet under natural light. PVA film (left) and W-CPDs/PVA film (right) under (e, f) natural light. (g) Inks under 365 nm UV light. (h, i) Films under 365 nm UV light.
Schematic diagram of the hydrothermal synthesis of W-CPD and its sensor mechanism for the detection of Fe (III).
Optical analysis to detect Fe (II)/Fe (III) ions.
Recovery of tap water spiked with Fe (III) detected with W-CPDs (n = 3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a: mean value of three determinations.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of tungsten-doped carbonated polymer dots with high sensitivity to Fe (III) and pH environments

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Material Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Material Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Material Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, 650093, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Provincial Key Lab on Bioinformatics, School of Computer Science and Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan 410083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Provincial Key Lab on Bioinformatics, School of Computer Science and Engineering, Central South University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hunan Provincial Key Lab on Bioinformatics, School of Computer Science and Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan 410083, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) have drawn a lot of attention in the past ten years because of their excellent selectivity and sensitivity. In this study, tungsten was doped into CPDs (W-CPDs) using a simple hydrothermal technique for the first time. With an excitation/emission maximum of 365/455 nm and excitation-independent emission, W-CPDs exhibit blue fluorescence and have an average particle diameter of 2.33 nm. Because of the dynamic quenching between Fe (III) and W-CPDs, there is a good linear relationship between the Fe (III) concentration and the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.012 μM for W-CPD fluorescence intensity. The intensity of the W-CPDs fluorescence is also found to be linearly correlated with pH values between 5.0 and 10.0. This technique enables quick detection of Fe (III) in drinking water and is expected to lower the risk of Fe (III) ingestion by humans. In addition, the creation of fluorescent flexible devices and information encryption are fascinating applications for W-CPDs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fig. 1 (a) W-CPDs growth mechanism. (b) W-O bond. (c) energy level of W-CPDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: As an industrial ferrous metal, iron is utilized in a variety of products, including abrasives, magnets, inks, pigments, and cores for motors and generators [1]. Water sources obtain iron by dissolving geological minerals or by dumping domestic and industrial wastewater, which the human body then absorbs directly through drinking water sources or indirectly through diet [2,3]. In biological systems, iron ions are necessary transition metal ions. For hematopoiesis, which is necessary for many physiological processes like iron storage, oxygen transport and metabolism, peroxidase and hemoglobin production [4]. However, numerous illnesses are caused by insufficient or excessive intake of iron in drinking water, including anemia, organ failure, diabetes, Parkinson's syndrome, and cancer [5]. Although atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), mass spectrometry, electrochemical methods, and inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) are frequently used to detect metal ions Fe (III) in tap water, the majority of these techniques are complex, with protracted test cycles and high instrument costs limiting their practical applications [6]. Due to the advancement of fluorescent nano-detection technology, it is now regarded as a straightforward, accurate, and cost-effective method.

As carbon nanoparticles with a particle size of less than or equal to 10 nm, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have a wide range of uses in nanotechnology, including optoelectronics, photocatalysis, bioimaging, and sensors [7]. The excellent properties of CQDs compared to other nanomaterials are mainly determined by their synthesis methods. For the top-down method, extended carbon structures or nanostructures like graphite, carbon fibers, nanotubes, and fullerenes serve as the precursors. These structures are then chemically or physically fragmented to produce very small carbon nanoparticles, which have the characteristic optical properties of CQDs [8]. Top-down synthesis methods are frequently challenging and time-consuming, and they frequently produce CQDs with low quantum yields. Very differently, the bottom-up synthesis method uses small molecules as precursors to be synthesized into N, S, P, and other heteroatom-doped CQDs by hydrothermal, microwave, pyrolysis, and acid dehydration methods [9]. Heteroatom doping can improve the energy band gap and electric structure of the CQDs, resulting in outstanding physical and chemical properties. Most CQDs prepared by bottom-up synthesis have been shown to be strictly defined as carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [10]. As a result, the bottom-up synthesis technique has the benefits of ease of use and elemental doping, and it can successfully satisfy the heteroatom doping of the CPDs [11].

Previous studies have shown that metal heteroatoms, particularly transition metal ions, have more electrons available than non-metal heteroatoms, which makes it simpler to modify the density and energy band gap of CQDs. Li’s [12] team uses chromium (III)-doped carbon dots as a fluorescent probe for p-Nitrophenol. Zhang’s [13] group successfully incorporated cobalt (II)-doped carbon dots for sensitive and accurate Cr fluorescence sensing. This has sparked our curiosity about studying metal atom doped CPDs. W which has a small ionic radius, a high electrical valence, and strong polarizability, is the most common form of tungsten element that belongs to the same group as chromium. Li’s [14] team used isoelectronic tungsten-doped monolayers in MoSe2 to achieve carrier modulation. He’s [15] group prepared multi-walled carbon nanotubes coated with defective tungsten oxide to enable deep oxidative desulfurization of diesel fuel. Zhang’s [16] group synthesized amorphous molybdenum sulfide tungsten/nitrogen-doped reduced graphene oxide nanocomposites by plasma treatment, providing a way to design high-performance HER catalysts. W-doped CPDs, however, have not been reported in prior studies.

In this work, W-CPDs with high sensitivity and selectivity is prepared by our one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid, urea, and Ammonium paratungstate hydrate (APT) as raw material ( Scheme 1). Notably, W-CPDs with an average size of only 2.33 nm is rich in surface groups, has a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.012 μM for Fe (III), a longer fluorescence lifetime, and pH sensitivity. It's exciting to note that W-CPDs also have a lot of promise for use in flexible fluorescent materials and information encryption inks.

All chemical reagents, including FeCl3·6H2O (98 %), CuCl2·2H2O (99 %), NaCl (99 %), AlCl3·6H2O (99 %), KNO3 (99 %), LiCl (99 %), NiCl2·6H2O (99.9 %), H3BO3 (99.5 %), C4H6MgO4 (99 %), C4H6MnO4 (99 %), Na2MoO4 (98 %), C4H6Co·4H2O (99 %), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), and urea (99 %) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Coꓻ Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The citric acid (99.5 %) and Ammonium Paratungstate (APT, 99.95 %) were bought from Shanghai Macklin Reagent Coꓻ Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All of the substances used in our experiment are of the analytical grade and have not been purified. Metal salt solution was configured with a resistance of 18.2 MΩ·cm deionized water.

W-CPDs was created using a one-step hydrothermal process [17,18]. In precisely, 90 ml of deionized water was combined with 3.18 g APT, 0.6 g urea, and 10 g citric acid, then the mixture was agitated using a magnetic mixer at room temperature for 30 min. The homogenous mixture was then put into a TGYF-A-micro reactor that was lined with Teflon and heated to 433 K for 12 h. The reddish-brown suspension was filtered over a 0.1 μM microporous membrane to get rid of the larger blue tungsten particles after it had cooled to room temperature. Then, to generate a purer W-CPDs sample, filtered W-CPDs solution was put into 500 kDa dialysis bags and dialyzed continuously for 24 h. W-CPDs solution was, therefore, freeze-dried for 36 h and made into a powder for further quantitative investigation. CPDs were produced using the identical synthesis procedures as W-CPDs, but without the introduction of APT.

The UV-VI absorption spectra of W-CPDs was discovered and assessed using the UV-1700PA UV absorption spectrometer (Shanghai Macylab Instrument Co. Ltd.). All of the fluorescence spectra were obtained using the fluorescence spectrophotometer F-7000 apparatus. To analyze the surface components and composition of W-CPDs, X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were generated by the K-Alpha spectrometer from Thermo Scientific. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer was employed to probe the chemical bonding information of powdered W-doped CPDs. The Raman spectra were collected using Thermo Scientific DXR 2xi Micro-Raman measurements using a 532 nm laser. The morphology of the powder W-CPDs samples was examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM). Quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime samples were detected by FLS1000, and their concentrations were 4.75 mg/ml and 2 mg/ml, respectively.

In a typical procedure, 0.2 g W-CPDs into 100 ml of deionized water, 5 ml of W-CPDs solution was taken to test its fluorescence intensity and was recorded its fluorescence intensity as F0. Subsequently, different Fe (III) concentration (0, 0.2, 0.4，0.6, 0.8, 1, 1.2, 1.4, 1.6, 1.8, 2, and 3 μM) were insert into W-CPDs (2 mg/ml) solution and shaken until homogeneous. The corresponding fluorescence spectra were measured by F-7000 spectrophotometer at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm (fluorescence intensity as F). At room temperature studies were also performed to compare the sensitivity of Fe (III) to 11 other metal ions.

The relative fluorescence quantum yield of W-CPDs and CPDs were calculated by the following equation:
(1)
Φ
S
=
Φ
R
Grad
S
Grad
R
η
S
2
η
R
2
Where Φ and Grad denote the quantum yield and integrated fluorescence intensity against absorbance, respectively. η represents the refractive index of the solvent in the atmosphere. The subscripts “R” and “S” indicate reference standard and samples, respectively. The flash quantum yield of deionized water measured at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm was chosen as reference standard.
η
S
/
η
R
is approximately equal to 1.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Fe (III) ions in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water from our lab (in Yunnan, China), was used to simulate real water samples by adding varying concentrations of Fe (III) ions. In light of this, the usefulness of W-CPDs was evaluated. Fluorescence intensity ratio (F0-F/F0) measured with the F-7000 spectrophotometer served as the basis for the detection of Fe (III) in water samples. W-CPDs were added to tap water to create a mixture with a concentration of 2 mg/ml that was ready for use in subsequent experiments. Ultimately, the deionized water ion detection method mentioned above was used to determine the Fe (III) ion content in tap water.

The morphology and dimensions of W-CPDs are characterized and investigated using TEM imaging. W-CPDs closely resemble spherical division with a statistical average diameter of 2.33 nm and a narrow particle size distribution in the range of 1.6–3.0 nm (see Fig. 1a inset). The lattice spacing is clearly observed in the high-resolution TEM image, and the lattice spacing of W-CPDs is 0.18 nm, corresponding to the (102) face of graphene (Fig. 1b) [19,20], further demonstrating that W-CPDs have highly crystallized carbon.

Raman spectra of CPDs and W-CPDs show two distinct peaks at 1360 cm−1 and 1568 cm−1, which correspond to the D band and G band, respectively (Fig. 1 c). In this experiment, where the ID/IG of W-CPDs is 0.901 and the ID/IG of CPDs is 0.708, the oxygen-containing defect on the surface of the CPDs is amplified by the doping of W elements [21].

CPDs and W-CPDs were compared using the exact chemical bonding information provided by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 1d). First, the FT-IR spectra of CPDs and W-CPDs show significant and broad peaks at 3200 cm−1 and 3021 cm−1 [22,23], confirming the presence of N-H stretching vibrations at 3200 cm−1 and C-H and O-H stretching vibrations at 3021 cm−1. Additionally, the 1701 cm−1 and 1396 cm−1 absorption peaks in W-CPDs are substantially more intense than they are in CPDs [24]. These absorption peaks are connected to the stretching vibrations of CO (1701 cm−1) and C-N (1396 cm−1). Due to the interaction between W and CPDs, more nitrogen elements are doped into CPDs as well as more oxygen-containing functional groups (carboxyl, carbonyl) are produced on the surface of CPDs [25]. After the absorption peak at 1623 cm−1 for the CC stretching vibration is also noticed, the absorption peak at 1186 cm−1 is attributed to the C-O stretching vibration. Another important clue indicating the W element was doped is the presence of W-O at 851 cm−1 absorption peaks, which can be seen in the spectra.

The surface components and composition of CPDs and W-CPDs were further examined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and their full spectra are displayed in Fig. 1e. CPD spectrum is composed of C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s at 283.3 eV, 398.4 eV, and 531.2 eV with estimated percentages of atoms of C-52.9 %, N-3.1 %, and O-44.0 %, respectively. In contrast, W-CPDs also include tungsten along with carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. In W-CPDs, the proportions of C, N, O, and W atoms are respectively 51.4 %, 5.2 %, 40.2 %, and 3.2 %. W4 f has a binding energy peak of 34.4 eV. This is due to the fact that addition of Ammonium Paratungstate (APT) to a synthesis process increases the amount of nitrogen element, and that when tungsten element changes the electron density of CDs during the doping process, forming more electron vacancy to facilitate the doping of nitrogen element.

The C 1 s XPS spectra in CPDs and W-CPDs are shown in Fig. 1f. These spectra can be divided into three peaks at 283.35 eV, 284.91 eV, and 287.25 eV, respectively, which correspond to three different functional groups of carbon: CC/C-C, C-O/C-N, and CO. 2020 [26]. The N 1 s functional groups in Pyrolitic-N, Pyridinic-N, and Graphite-N are represented by the three peaks in the N 1 s spectrum shown in Fig. 1g, respectively [27]. CPDs and W-CPDs may both be seen in the corresponding O 1 s spectra since W-CPDs are present at the peak 528.60 eV for C-O (Fig. 1h) [28,29].

They both have functional groups CO, C-OH/O-C-O with peaks at 530.18 eV and 531.86 eV, respectively. The combination of the C 1 s and O 1 s spectra reveals that W interacts with C and O when the CO concentration in W-CPDs is reduced and C-O is identified [30]. The W 4 f spectra of W-CPDs exhibit four peaks at 32.24 eV, 33.90 eV, 36.07 eV, and 39.51 eV, which are associated with the W 4 f states in W-O, W 4 f7/2, W 4 f5/2, and the loss feature (Fig. 1i).

Further investigation of the optical properties of different CPDs by UV-VIs absorption spectroscopy and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy ( Fig. 2a). As shown in the inset in Fig. 2a, W-CPDs has good dispersion in deionized water and emits blue light when exposed to a 365 nm UV lamp. The curves of CPDs and W-CPDs in UV-VIs absorption spectra are similar, except that intensity of the absorption peaks of W-CPDs is enhanced. Moreover, distinctive characteristic peaks in the spectrum are shown at 330 nm and 355 nm, which correspond to π-π * transition and n-π * transition, respectively. Significant differences in CPDs and W-CPDs fluorescence intensity are demonstrated in excitation and emission PL spectra (Fig. 2b). The best emission was then examined between 335 and 395 nm (Fig. 2c). The emission peak of W-CPDs, however, remained constant around 455 nm regardless of the excitation wavelength, and the best emission spectra were obtained at excitation wavelengths of 365 nm. W-CPDs exhibit non-excited emission at the stable emission wavelength due to their uniform size and solid surface condition, which also help to prevent autofluorescence in use. The excitation wavelength employed in the subsequent tests will therefore be 365 nm. Since W-CPDs are predicted to have a higher quantum yield than CPDs (3.86 % and 2.67 %, respectively), it has been determined that W doping reduces the energy band gap of CPDs [31]. The − 5.42 mV potential of W-CPDs is 4.45 times lower than that of − 24.10 mV of CPDs, which further proves that the doping of W element successfully improves the potential of CPDs.

We hypothesize that W-CPDs can distinguish between various metal ions thanks to the variety of carboxyl and amino groups on their surface. For verification of the high selectivity of W-CPDs for Fe (III), the metal ions Al3+, Cu2+, K+, Li+, Na+, Ni2+, Fe3+, B3+, Co2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, and Mo6+ were added at a content of 12 μM under the same circumstances [32]. When W-CPDs were exposed to various metal ions, as shown in Fig. 3 a, the relative strength of Fe3+ does not decrease or inverse increase due to our calculation formula (F0-F)/F0. Where F0 represents the PL strength of W-CPDs solution when no metal ions are added at all, and F represents the PL strength of W-CPDs solution after the addition of metal ions, only Fe (III) experienced a significant fluorescence quenching. For the other cations, no discernible signal changes occurred. Thus, the extraordinary ferric selectivity of W-CPDs was unequivocally proven.

The examination of the sensitivity of 2 mg/ml W-CPDs to Fe (III) was carried out by adding different concentrations of Fe (III). As can be seen in Fig. 3b, the PL intensity of W-CPDs at 455 nm drop as the amount of Fe increased (III). We utilized the (F0-F)/F0 formula to fit the data and generate the graph (inset of Fig. 3b) using the PL intensities produced at 455 nm for varied concentrations of Fe (III) content between 0 and 1.0 μM. The relationship between PL intensities and Fe (III) concentration is then clearly seen to be linear, with Y= 0.5346X+ 0.0365 and R2 = 0.99. The limit of detection (LOD) for this extraordinarily sensitive Fe (III) experimental setup has been determined to be 0.012 μM [33]. We complemented the experimental data by showing the CPDs sensing limit of detection (LOD) of 0.56 μM with Fe (III) added in Fig. 3d, which is a hundred times higher than W-CPDs limit of detection (0.012 μM). It is further confirmed that the addition of W elements enhances the sensitivity of W-CPDs to Fe (III).

Table 1 compares the sensitivity of our investigation (III) with those of earlier reports. It is intriguing that the prepared W-CPDs not only had a lower LOD than most of the report nanoprobes, but also the results were lower than 0.3 mg/L in the Chinese sanitary standards for domestic drinking water [13]. Obviously, Fe (III) induced W-CPDs fluorescence quenching. W-CPDs have many carboxyl and amino groups on their surface, which work together to form a stable chelate with Fe (III). The creation of chelates altered the structural distribution of excitons in W-CPDs by promoting the nonradiative recombination of excitons through the charge transfer mechanism, which leads to the generation of electron-hole complex annihilation and the quenching of fluorescence.

To further ascertain whether W element doping lengthens the fluorescence lifespan of CPDs, the addition of Fe (III) with or without triggering the electron-hole complex annihilation of W-CPDs was studied. The average fluorescence lifetimes for CPDs and W-CPDs were calculated using curve-fitting, and they are shown in Fig. 3c to be 7.72 ns and 8.19 ns, respectively. Another demonstration of the W element doping with CPDs' near-improving fluorescence lifetime. The average fluorescence lifetime of W-CPDs dropped to 7.09 ns and showed dynamic quenching with the addition of Fe (III). Additionally, charge transfer and exciton recombination have been seen in W-CPDs + Fe (III) systems.

In the potential generation of W-CPDs depicted in Fig. 4a, the binding site of W (VI) ions is identified using experimental characterization, fluorescence lifetimes, and quantum yields. There are three basic steps used to create W-CPDs. To create W-citric acid complexes, W (VI) ions first polymerize with the carboxyl groups of citric acid. Smaller fluorophores can only develop at lower temperatures and for a shorter amount of time. A graphene backbone is created as a result of intermolecular dehydration, which enhances W (VI) binding to tiny fluorophores even more.

The amino and carboxyl groups then continue to cross-link and polymerize to enclose W (VI) in the polymer, resulting in continuous carbonization of the polymer with increasing reaction temperature and time, allowing W (VI) to enter carbon nuclei through W-O bonds. Small molecules derived from nitrogen are added, which initiates the final step. As shown in Fig. 4b, the connection of the W-O bond increases the sp2 effective content. Furthermore, as can be seen from Fig. 4c, the formation of W-O bonds in CPDs not only compensates for oxygen vacancies in CPDs to narrow the energy band gap and prevent non-radiative recombination, but also increases the effective content of sp2, promotes electron transfer, raises quantum yield, and lengthens the lifetime of fluorescence in W-CPDs.

The W-CPDs prepared in this work showed good pH sensitivity. Fig. 5a shows how the fluorescence intensity of W-CPDs changes as the pH is raised from 3.0 to 12.0 by adding NaOH drops at a time. Due to the high levels of acidity and alkalinity, there is little chance for PL intensity to fluctuate, yet pH values between 5.0 and 10.0 have been proven to display excellent linearity (Fig. 5b). Additionally, the R2 = 0.982 correlation coefficient of the linear regression equation demonstrated that it was well fitted.

We assumed that variable pH (from 5.0 to 10.0) PL intensity fluctuations were due to W-CPDs deprotonation based on the aforementioned experiment. Deprotonation of W-CPDs increased when pH rose from 5.0 to 10.0, and more carboxyl groups were formed on the W-CPD surface, which raised the PL intensity [39]. The reduced fluorescence intensity of W-CPDs at pH 12 is attributed to the amino protonation of the surface, which increases the ability of intramolecular electron transfer between -NH3 + and W-CPDs as pH rises to 12, resulting in PL quenching. Hence, water source identification may be done using the improved pH-sensitive fluorescence probes of the W-CPDs.

For real sample detection, tap water was treated in accordance with the experimental part of the process. Tap water samples were then injected with Fe (III) ions at various concentrations (0.2, 0.6, and 1.0 μM) in order to be detected (see Table 2). Measured concentrations and added concentrations were very similar. Furthermore, the recoveries were nearly 98.2 %. In conclusion, we have created novel nano-sensing materials that are easy to use, highly accurate, and environmentally friendly. These materials have many potential applications in the testing of actual water samples.

Over the duration of 1–30 days, the fluorescence intensity of the W-CPDs solution gradually decreased, although it was still 92 % on the final day ( Fig. 6a). As shown in Fig. 6b, W-CPDs solution appeared pale yellow when seen in natural light. We observed the evolution of the fluorescence intensity of the W-CPD solution. After 30 days of observation, the solution still emits a bright blue light at 365 nm UV light (Fig. 6c). The findings show that W components help CPDs to sustain PL strength and lengthen their service lives.

We created W-CPDs, which is an ink that resembles fluorescent. W-CPDs were added to homemade inks that were used to paint a cellulose filler membrane. We noticed that the image was not clear under normal lighting conditions (Fig. 6d), but it was clear under UV light at 365 nm (Fig. 6g) and could be partially quenched with Fe (III) solution. W-CPDs have a lot of potential for use in fluorescent inks for information encryption.

Under natural light, composite film made using W-CPDs combined with PVA was essentially identical to regular PVA film (Fig. 6e). In contrast to common PVA film, which lacked any discernible fluorescence, composite film emits a bright blue light when exposed to 365 nm UV light (Fig. 6h). This demonstrates the fluorescence of the composite film succession W-CPDs. Additionally, when we compared two films after they had been bent, both displayed excellent flexibility (Fig. 6f), and the composite film that had been bent still displayed good fluorescence when exposed to 365 nm UV light (Fig. 6 i). The use of W-CPDs and PVA films in fluorescent flexible devices is therefore highly promising.

W-CPDs was synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid, urea and APT. They are usually 2.33 nm in size and have a very stable surface rich in amino and carboxyl functional groups. The Fe (III) detection limit (LOD) is determined to be 0.012 μM and the linear range is 0.2–1.0 μM, which is highly selective for Fe (III). Due to their good recovery in real water samples and exceptionally high sensitivity for pH, W-CPDs have shown significant potential as fluorescent nanosensors for pH and Fe (III) detection. Additionally, they have shown great promise for data encryption, flexible luminous electronics, and fluorescent inks.

Yushu Han (First Author): Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft; Hongdong Li: Data curation, Writing – original draft; Liang Liu: Visualization, Investigation. Shuyu Huang: Resources, Supervision. Zhaojie Li: Resources, Supervision; Wenfu Zhang: Software, Validation; Deqi Min: Software, Validation; Rui Bao: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Jianhong Yi (Corresponding Author); Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52064032, 52174345), the Yunnan Science and Technology Projects (Grant No. 202202AG050004, 202002AB080001).
